title: Brakes & clutches
summary: A focused worked-problem lesson on friction brakes and clutches: pad (plane) brakes pivoted and non-pivoted, drum (shoe) brakes, self-energizing band brakes with the $e^{f\theta}$ tension ratio, disc brakes, plus plane/multi-plate and conical clutches. The unifying method is normal force from a lever balance ŌåÆ friction force $T=fN$ ŌåÆ torque ŌåÆ dynamics. Eight fully solved exam problems (33ŌĆō40) reproduce the answer-key values.
objectives: Find the normal force from a brake lever's moment balance and recognise self-energizing vs self-de-energizing configurations
Compute braking/transmitted torque for pad, drum, band, disc, plate-clutch and conical-clutch geometries
Apply the band-brake capstan relation $T_1/T_2=e^{f\theta}$ with ╬Ė in radians
Connect braking torque to dynamics: deceleration, stopping time/distance, transmitted power and dissipated heat
Determine pivot/support reactions by vector-balancing all forces on a brake lever or shoe
Size clutch radii and back out contact pressures from the uniform-wear / max-pressure conditions

blocks.text: Brakes & clutches ŌĆö the friction-machine family

Brakes and clutches all do the same physical thing: a **normal force N** is pressed onto a moving surface, dry friction produces a **tangential force T = f┬ĘN**, and that friction force times its radius gives a **braking (or transmitted) torque**. The whole topic is bookkeeping: (1) find the normal force from a lever/actuator balance, (2) multiply by f to get the friction force, (3) take moments to get torque, and (4) use dynamics (KE, $C=I\dot\omega$) to connect torque to stopping time, deceleration or transmitted power.

On every friction surface the tangential force is $T=f\,N$ (slip) or limited by $f_a N$ (stick). A brake torque about the rotation axis is $M_f=T\cdot r=f\,N\,r$, where $r$ is the radius at which friction acts (drum radius, mean disc radius, etc.).
Pad (plane) brakes ŌĆö pivoted vs non-pivoted

A pad/block is pushed against a flat or cylindrical surface by a lever. Write the **moment balance of the lever about its pivot**. The applied force F, the normal reaction N and the friction force T=fN each contribute a moment. The friction moment is what makes the brake **self-energizing** (T helps press the pad harder ŌåÆ less F needed) or **self-de-energizing** (T fights the actuator ŌåÆ more F needed). Which one you get depends on the rotation/sliding direction, so a brake almost always must be solved twice (one for each direction).

$\ell_F$ = arm of the applied force, $\ell_N$ = arm of the normal force, $c$ = perpendicular distance of the friction line from the pivot. Minus sign = self-energizing (smaller denominator, larger N), plus sign = self-de-energizing.

Master recipe: **N from a lever moment balance ŌåÆ T = fN ŌåÆ torque or braking force ŌåÆ dynamics.** The pin/pivot reaction R is then found by vector-summing all forces on the lever (it is NOT simply N or F).

Two curved shoes press outward (or a band inward) on a drum of radius $r_d$. Each shoe is driven by a lever exactly like a pad: $F\,\ell_F=N\,\ell_N\mp f N c$. For the two shoes of one drum, one is leading (self-energizing, smaller denominator ŌåÆ larger N) and one is trailing (larger denominator ŌåÆ smaller N). Total braking torque is $M_f=f\,(N_1+N_2)\,r_d$. A **pivoted** shoe pivots about a fixed point on the shoe; a **non-pivoted (rigid)** shoe is rigidly attached to its lever ŌĆö the algebra of the moment balance is the same, only the arms differ.
Band brakes and the e^{f╬Ė} ratio

A flexible band wraps an angle ╬Ė around the drum. As in belt/capstan theory the two end tensions obey the Eytelwein (capstan) relation. The tight side $T_1$ and slack side $T_2$ differ by the exponential of $f\theta$, and the braking torque is $(T_1-T_2)r$.

$T_1$ is always the larger (tight) tension on the side toward which the drum surface pulls the band. Swapping the rotation direction swaps which physical band end is $T_1$, which is exactly why band brakes are strongly self-energizing in one sense and not the other.

Classic mistakes: (1) using the wrong sign of the friction moment ŌåÆ solving the wrong direction; (2) forgetting that $T_1/T_2=e^{f\theta}$ uses ╬Ė in **radians** (180┬░ = ŽĆ); (3) for the pin reaction, writing R = N (it must balance F, N and T together); (4) a disc-brake caliper presses on the disc but each caliper has ONE friction interface per side ŌĆö count surfaces carefully; (5) mixing slip f and stick $f_a$.

An axial pad presses on a flat rotor. Friction torque $M_f=f\,F\,R_m$ per friction surface, with mean radius $R_m=\tfrac{R_i+R_e}{2}$ (uniform wear). For $N$ friction surfaces $M_f=N\,f\,F\,R_m$. No self-energizing in the simple axial pad.

A clutch transmits torque by friction, same physics as a disc brake. Flat multi-plate: $C=N\,f\,F\,R_m$ with $N$ active surfaces, $R_m=\tfrac{R_i+R_e}{2}$. Conical clutch (half-angle ╬▒): the normal force is amplified, $Q=F/\sin\alpha$, so $C=\dfrac{f\,F\,R_m}{\sin\alpha}$ ŌĆö a small axial force gives a large torque.

blocks.tex: \text{Uniform wear: } p\,r=\text{const}\;\Rightarrow\; p_{max}=\frac{c}{R_i},\; p_{min}=\frac{c}{R_e},\quad F=2\pi c\,(R_e-R_i)
blocks.text: Pressure is highest at the inner radius. $c=p\,r=F/[2\pi(R_e-R_i)]$. This lets you back out $p_{max},p_{min}$ once F is known. (Uniform-pressure variant: $F=\pi p (R_e^2-R_i^2)$.)

steps.label: 1. Dynamics target
steps.text: From the required deceleration/stopping time/power, get the braking torque or friction force you must produce: $M_f=I\dot\omega$ with $I=m\rho^2$ and $\dot\omega=\omega_0/t$; or braking force $T=m\,a$; or transmitted torque $C=P/\omega$.

steps.label: 2. Relate torque to N
steps.text: Pad/drum: $M_f=fN r$. Disc/clutch: $C=N f F R_m$. Cone: $C=fFR_m/\sin\alpha$. Band: $M_f=(T_1-T_2)r$ with $T_1/T_2=e^{f\theta}$.

steps.label: 3. Lever moment balance
steps.text: Solve the actuator force from $F\,\ell_F=N\,\ell_N\mp fNc$. Do it once for each sliding direction (sign of the friction moment flips).

steps.label: 4. Reactions
steps.text: Find pin/support reactions by vector force balance on the lever/shoe (sum of F, N, T).

steps.label: 5. Dissipation
steps.text: Heat = lost kinetic energy: $L_i=\tfrac12 I\omega_0^2$ (or $\tfrac12 mv^2$); dissipated power $P_d=T\,v$ or $M_f\,\omega$.

question.prompt: In a band brake wrapping ╬Ė=180┬░ with f=0.3, what is the tight-to-slack tension ratio $T_1/T_2$?
question.explanation: ╬Ė must be in radians: 180┬░ = ŽĆ rad, so $T_1/T_2=e^{f\theta}=e^{0.3\pi}\approx2.57$. Option D wrongly uses degrees; A drops the angle.

question.prompt: Why does a conical clutch transmit much more torque than a flat clutch for the same axial force F?

options.content: The cone has a larger mean radius automatically
The wedge action makes the normal force $Q=F/\sin\alpha$, larger than F
Friction coefficient is higher on cones

question.explanation: On a cone of half-angle ╬▒ the axial force is reacted by a normal force $Q=F/\sin\alpha$; with small ╬▒ this is much larger than F, so $C=fFR_m/\sin\alpha$. The friction coefficient and radius are not intrinsically different.
